exercises.title: I-Statement Transformation
exercises.description: Convert each blame statement into an I-statement using the formula: 'I feel [emotion] when [specific behavior] because [impact on you].'
hints: Start with 'I feel...' not 'I think...' or 'I believe...'
Describe the BEHAVIOR, not the PERSON
Explain WHY it matters to you
scenarios: Original: 'You never listen to my ideas in meetings.'
Original: 'You're always late and it's so disrespectful.'
Original: 'You don't care about this project like I do.'
Original: 'You made that mistake on purpose to make me look bad.'
exercises.solution: Rewritten: (1) 'I feel unheard when my suggestions aren't acknowledged in meetings because I want to contribute to our team's success.' (2) 'I feel anxious when meetings start late because it impacts my schedule and I end up rushing.' (3) 'I feel concerned about the project timeline because I want us both to succeed and I need more collaboration.' (4) 'I feel frustrated by the error because it affects our client relationship — can we discuss what happened?'

exercises.title: The Difficult Conversation Prep Sheet
exercises.description: Choose a real difficult conversation you need to have. Complete this preparation sheet before the conversation.
hints: Be specific — vague preparation leads to vague conversations
Write it down — it helps organize your thoughts
Include their perspective, not just yours

prepSheet.situation: [What specific behavior or situation needs to be addressed? Be factual.]
prepSheet.impact: [What is the impact on you, the team, or the project? Facts + feelings.]
prepSheet.desiredOutcome: [What do you want the result to be? Be specific.]
prepSheet.theirPerspective: [What might they be feeling? What reasons might they have?]
prepSheet.openingLine: [How will you start the conversation? Use an I-statement or common ground.]

exercises.solution: Example completed sheet: Situation: 'The last three reports were submitted after the Friday deadline.' Impact: 'This delays the Monday leadership meeting and creates weekend work for me.' Desired outcome: 'Reports submitted by Thursday EOD.' Their perspective: 'Maybe they're overloaded, or unclear on the urgency, or the process needs streamlining.' Opening: 'I want to make sure we're aligned on the reporting timeline — can we discuss?'

exercises.title: De-escalation Role Play
exercises.description: Practice de-escalation with a partner. One person plays a frustrated colleague; the other practices calming techniques. Switch roles after 3 minutes.
hints: The 'frustrated' person should escalate — raise voice, use blame statements
The 'calm' person should use: acknowledgment, slow pacing, questions, finding agreement
Don't try to solve the problem until the emotional temperature drops
deescalationScript: Step 1: Acknowledge — 'I can see you're really frustrated about this.'
Step 2: Slow down — Lower your voice, speak slowly, pause between sentences.
Step 3: Find agreement — 'We both want this project to succeed.'
Step 4: Ask — 'Help me understand what's most concerning for you.'
Step 5: Collaborate — 'What would help? What can we do differently?'
exercises.solution: After 3 minutes, most 'frustrated' actors report feeling heard when the other person acknowledges their emotion first. The key insight: you can't solve a problem until the person feels understood. De-escalation isn't weakness — it's the fastest path to resolution.
